title_en: Taharah in Al-Muqaddimaat: Maliki Purification Principles
title_ar: الطهارة في المقدمات: أصول الطهارة المالكية
content_en:
Ibn Rushd the Elder's introduction to taharah in Al-Muqaddimaat establishes the foundational principles of Maliki purification law before the student encounters the detailed rulings of the Mudawwanah. His approach is characteristically systematic: he identifies the sources of disagreement among scholars, explains the Maliki position and its basis, and distinguishes what is agreed upon from what is disputed.

Ibn Rushd begins by establishing the importance of taharah as a condition for the validity of prayer and certain other acts of worship. The Quranic command 'O you who believe, when you intend to pray, wash your faces and your hands' (al-Ma'idah 5:6) establishes that purification is a condition, not a virtue — prayer performed without it is not merely deficient but invalid. This framing of taharah as a condition of validity rather than a recommended preparation is the foundation of all subsequent taharah law.

On the question of water, Ibn Rushd explains the Maliki position that water remains pure unless one of its characteristics actually changes due to impurity. This applies to both small and large amounts of water — the Maliki school does not use the two-qullah threshold to automatically render small amounts of water impure. He notes the hadith 'Water is pure and nothing makes it impure' as the foundational text, and explains that this general principle is qualified only by the specific text showing that water which has changed due to impurity is no longer usable for purification.

The Maliki distinction on musta'mal water (water used for an obligatory purification) is explained: unlike the Hanafi and Shafi'i schools, the Maliki school holds that musta'mal water retains its purifying capacity — it can be used again for a second purification. Ibn Rushd explains this by noting the absence of any specific text removing the purifying capacity of used water, combined with the general Maliki principle that water's purity is the default.

For wudu, Ibn Rushd introduces the seven Maliki obligations: intention, washing the face, washing the arms, wiping the entire head, wiping the ears (as an extension of head wiping), washing the feet, and continuity (muwalat). He explains why the Maliki school requires continuity (the acts must be performed without unreasonable interruption) but not order (tartib) — the evidence for continuity from prophetic practice is stronger than the evidence for a specific required sequence, in the Maliki view.

The rubbing (dalk) obligation in the Maliki school's ghusl is explained with particular care: Ibn Rushd notes that the Maliki scholars derived this obligation from the prophetic practice of actively washing (not merely pouring water over the body) and from the linguistic meaning of the Quranic command to 'purify' (tatahhar), which implies active engagement rather than passive wetting.

Ibn Rushd's treatment of the female prayer times and the start and end of menstruation and postnatal bleeding introduces students to the Maliki school's careful and detailed rules on these matters, which have significant practical importance for women's worship obligations. His systematic introduction to these topics before the detailed Mudawwanah discussions prepares students to engage with the more complex rulings intelligently.
